items.q: What is the purpose of database normalisation?
items.correct: To reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies.
items.misconception: To make the database run faster.
items.random_wrong: To make the database look neater.
items.why: Students think normalisation is about performance. In fact, heavy normalisation often slows queries. Its purpose is DATA INTEGRITY - preventing inconsistencies by storing each fact once.

items.q: What does an INNER JOIN return?
items.correct: Only rows with matching values in both tables.
items.misconception: All rows from both tables combined.
items.random_wrong: Only the inner columns of each table.
items.why: Students confuse INNER (intersection) with FULL OUTER (union). INNER drops unmatched rows from BOTH sides; LEFT keeps all from left; RIGHT keeps all from right; FULL OUTER keeps everything.

items.q: What is the main advantage of asymmetric encryption?
items.correct: Secure key exchange without sharing a secret in advance.
items.misconception: It is faster than symmetric encryption.
items.why: Students think asymmetric is just 'better'. It's actually SLOWER than symmetric. Its advantage is solving key distribution - the public key can be shared openly without compromising security.

items.q: Codd's relational model was published in?
items.why: It's 1970. “1950” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is NOT part of ACID?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Availability. “Atomicity” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many tables are typically joined in a SQL INNER JOIN at once (minimum)?
items.why: The real figure is 2 tables. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
